title: Dad's Puttanesca
summary:
Dad's Puttanesca recipe is a simple yet delicious dish inspired by the flavours of sugo alla puttanesca. This classic Neapolitan recipe is a staple in the Mediterranean diet, featuring a savoury mix of anchovies, garlic, olives, and capers in a tomato-based sauce. Unlike bolognese sauce, it relies on pantry ingredients, making it perfect for quick pasta dishes.

Ideal for lovers of Italian food, this sauced dish pairs beautifully with common pasta like spaghetti or fresh pasta. To enhance the flavours, sauté the garlic and anchovies until fragrant, then mix in the tomatoes. Sprinkle some fresh parsley on top for an authentic Italian touch.

While it’s a tomato sauce, it contains less trans fat than creamy options like carbonara. To add a Mediterranean twist, pair it with a niçoise salad or even pita bread. Low in saturated fat and rich in seasoning, it's a great option for balanced meals.

method.description: Add your chopped anchovies and chilli flakes into a frying pan with a tablespoon of olive oil and a tbsp of oil from the anchovy tin. Fry for one minute.
Crush your garlic cloves and add with a large handful of finely chopped parsley stalks. Mix everything together.
Add your capers, your olives and pour in the plum tomatoes. Break the tomatoes up with a wooden spoon. Refill both the tins halfway with water and add to the sauce.

Allow the sauce to reduce. Add a couple of tablespoons of your starchy pasta water to make it nice and glossy. Just when you are removing the pasta from the heat, season the sauce with salt and pepper and add a large handful of chopped parsley.

Drain the pasta and pour it into the saucepan with the puttanesca sauce. Mix everything together and remove from the heat. Serve with some parsley leaves scattered on top and a good glug of olive oil. Enjoy!

chefs.name: Ben Lebus
chefs.summary: Ben Lebus is the CEO and founder of Mob. Ben has always been passionate about food and learnt to cook from his father, who ran an Italian restaurant for a number of years. You can regularly find him cooking (and eating) some sort of puttanesca.
